title: Vanilla icebox cookies
subtitle: Slice-and-bake refrigerator cookies with a sandy vanilla crumb
excerpt: Icebox cookies, also called refrigerator cookies, are formed as a log, chilled until firm, then sliced into rounds and baked. The method gives very consistent, uniformly thin biscuits with a short, sandy crumb. The dough can be refrigerated for days before baking.
sourceNotes: Icebox cookies are documented in American home-baking literature from the 1930s onward, when domestic refrigerators became widespread. The slice-and-bake method is described in numerous public-domain American cookbooks from that era.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unbaked dough log, well wrapped. Slice and bake from frozen, adding 2 to 3 extra minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe; form two logs and bake on separate trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough log keeps in the fridge for up to 5 days before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 160 degrees C; the edges should be lightly golden before removing

glossaryTerms.definition: The icebox method forms biscuit dough into a cylinder, wraps it tightly in cling film, and refrigerates it until firm. The chilled log can then be sliced into uniform rounds with a sharp knife, giving evenly sized biscuits with no need for rolling or cutting. The cold fat sets firmly, which helps each round hold its shape without spreading in the oven. The method also means the dough can be prepared days in advance.

content.text: The appeal of icebox cookies is the method as much as the flavour. The

means the dough can sit in the fridge for up to five days, ready to slice and bake whenever needed. The biscuits are short and sandy rather than chewy; the high butter ratio and icing sugar produce a very fine, almost crumbling crumb. A vanilla pod scraped into the dough makes a noticeable difference over extract.

items.prepNote: 2 tsp, or the seeds of 1 vanilla pod

content.text: Beat the softened butter and icing sugar together until pale and smooth. Beat in the egg yolks and vanilla. Add the flour and salt and mix until the dough just comes together into a soft, smooth mass. Turn out onto a sheet of cling film and shape into a cylinder about 5 cm in diameter. Roll the cling film around it tightly, twisting the ends to compress the log into a uniform shape. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours, until firm.

Preheat the oven to 180 degrees C (conventional). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Remove the log from the fridge and slice into 7 mm rounds with a sharp knife. Place on the trays, spacing about 3 cm apart. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the edges are lightly golden and the centres are set. Leave on the trays for 3 minutes before transferring to a rack. The biscuits firm considerably as they cool.
